patterns.summary: Recursive forced deletion — removes files permanently without confirmation.
patterns.suggestion: Use 'rm -ri' for interactive confirmation, or 'trash' to move to Trash.

patterns.summary: Recursive deletion — removes directory trees.
patterns.suggestion: Double-check the target path. Consider 'rm -ri' for confirmation.

patterns.summary: Deleting root, home, or everything in current directory.
patterns.suggestion: This is almost certainly not what you want. Be very specific about the target.

patterns.summary: Recursively setting world-readable/writable/executable permissions.
patterns.suggestion: Use specific permissions (e.g., 755 for dirs, 644 for files). Avoid 777.

patterns.summary: Setting world-readable/writable/executable permissions.
patterns.suggestion: 755 (dirs) or 644 (files) is usually what you need. 777 is a security risk.

patterns.summary: Downloading and executing code directly — you can't inspect it first.
patterns.suggestion: Download the script first, review it, then execute: curl -o script.sh URL && less script.sh && bash script.sh

patterns.summary: Writing directly to a device — can overwrite your disk.
patterns.suggestion: Triple-check the target device. 'of=/dev/sda' will destroy your system disk.

patterns.summary: Recursive forced deletion with root privileges — maximum damage potential.
patterns.suggestion: Be extremely careful. Consider running without sudo first to see what would be deleted.

patterns.summary: Changing ownership or permissions with root privileges.
patterns.suggestion: Ensure you're targeting the correct path. Recursive changes can break your system.

patterns.summary: Force push overwrites remote history — other collaborators will lose work.
patterns.suggestion: Use 'git push --force-with-lease' instead — it fails if someone else pushed.

patterns.summary: Hard reset discards all uncommitted changes permanently.
patterns.suggestion: Consider 'git stash' first to save your changes, or use 'git reset --soft'.

patterns.summary: Removes untracked files permanently.
patterns.suggestion: Run 'git clean -n' first (dry run) to see what would be deleted.

patterns.summary: Starts an HTTP server that exposes the current directory to the network.
patterns.suggestion: Bind to localhost only: python -m http.server --bind 127.0.0.1

patterns.summary: Exposes a local service to the public internet.
patterns.suggestion: Make sure you know what service you're exposing and who can access it.

patterns.summary: Printing sensitive environment variables — may appear in logs or history.
patterns.suggestion: Avoid echoing secrets. Use 'printenv VAR | wc -c' to check if set without exposing.

patterns.summary: Setting secrets as environment variables — will appear in shell history.
patterns.suggestion: Use a .env file or secrets manager instead. Prefix with a space to avoid history (if HISTCONTROL=ignorespace).

patterns.summary: Creating a filesystem destroys all data on the target partition.
patterns.suggestion: Verify the target device with 'lsblk' before running. This is irreversible.

patterns.summary: Fork bomb — spawns processes until the system is unresponsive.
patterns.suggestion: This will crash your system. If run accidentally, use 'killall' from another terminal.

patterns.summary: Securely overwrites files — data is unrecoverable after shred.
patterns.suggestion: Verify the target. Unlike 'rm', shred data cannot be recovered.

patterns.summary: Wipes filesystem signatures from a device.
patterns.suggestion: Verify the device with 'lsblk'. This makes partitions unrecognizable.

patterns.summary: Operating on SSH keys — may expose or overwrite key material.
patterns.suggestion: Back up your keys before modifying. Never share private keys.

patterns.summary: Transferring files over SSH — verify destination and direction.
patterns.suggestion: Double-check source and destination to avoid overwriting remote files.

patterns.summary: Modifying firewall rules — may lock you out or expose services.
patterns.suggestion: Test rules with a timeout: 'iptables -A ... && sleep 60 && iptables -D ...'

patterns.summary: Installing Python packages system-wide can break OS dependencies.
patterns.suggestion: Use a virtual environment: 'python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate'

patterns.summary: Upgrading all Homebrew packages at once — may introduce breaking changes.
patterns.suggestion: Upgrade specific packages: 'brew upgrade <package>'

patterns.summary: Writing to system configuration files — may break your system.
patterns.suggestion: Always back up before modifying: 'sudo cp /etc/file /etc/file.bak'

patterns.summary: Modifying authentication or sudo configuration.
patterns.suggestion: Use 'visudo' (not a direct editor) to avoid locking yourself out.

patterns.summary: Removes all unused Docker resources — may delete data volumes.
patterns.suggestion: Run without '-a' first, or 'docker system df' to see what would be removed.

patterns.summary: Running container in privileged mode — full host access.
patterns.suggestion: Avoid --privileged unless strictly necessary. Use --cap-add for specific capabilities.

patterns.summary: Reading shell history — may contain passwords and sensitive commands.
patterns.suggestion: Grep for specific entries instead of dumping the entire history.

patterns.summary: Reading sensitive credential or key files.
patterns.suggestion: Be careful with output — don't pipe to logs or paste into chats.
